transcript.transcript: Today is May 4th, 1977, and I'm going to interview Maydale Clements. The general topic will be Northern Idaho. Mr. Clements, where were you born? I was born in Daga, Norway, on April 7th, 1908. How long have you lived in Smotherville? I lived here in Smotherville a long year over two days and a half. First time I came here was 1931. I lived here a short time, a few years later in 1947. I've lived here for the last 30 years. Where were your parents born? They were born in Bergen, Norway. What was your occupation? My father's occupation, he was a cook. He came to this area of the country when he was a very young man. And he also went to McCool Rush, Alaska. And he came to the Fort Lanes Mining District the first time in right around the turn of the century, I'd say about 1890. Okay, when we first talked you said something about priests coming into this area? Yes. You get a historic reflection of North Idaho, mostly taking in the county of Kootenai and Shoshone. By the early history, it relates a great deal to the efforts of the Jesuit priests and their missionaries. And some of the early dealings with the Coeur d'Alene Indians, we find that they were a warlike tribe of people. They were not hospitable, and the Hudson Bay Trading Company had never any time been able to establish a trading post in their area. While to the further north of them were the Salish Indians of Montana. They had went back east and obtained the services of the Jesuit priests. And Father D. Smith, when he was traveling from the Salish Indians to Vancouver in the year of 1842. He stopped off on the shores of Lake Port Lane, approximately right at the same point where the North Idaho Junior College is located today. There was a large Indian encampment there. And this here camp, the Indians, bore a name that was spelled N-C-H-I-M. That was a separate name. I don't know how it would be pronounced in the Chim. And the second word was Kilgos, K-I-L-G-O-S. And it was located at the head of the Spokane River. Fort Sherman was also located at the same point that a later date was determined. And here they erected a log chapel. This is the first mission house built in the wilderness that I know of. It was only a temporary structure, but it served the intent of the meet and the purposes of Father DeSmet, because he was not going to be able to stay with them over a very long period of time. His time was very limited, and he had to hurry on to Vancouver. But he did leave in the short period of time that he was there a very lasting impression upon the Coeur d'Alene Indians. And it was a result of his teachings and the influences of the so-called black folks that the Coeur d'Alene Indians became acquainted with the gospel of the white man. While he was there, He taught them some of the dogma of his church, some of the prayers, the Hail Marys, the Acts of Confession, Ten Commandments, and other prayers that were common to the church. He adopted a very clever manner in teaching his Indian children. He lined them up and told each one to remember his position. And then each time he lined them up for each one to return back to the same position. And to each Indian child, he gave them exactly one sentence to learn. And then as they spoke, each one said their sentence and had made a complete prayer. And in a short time, the whole village could say the prayer. Very simple and ingenious method of teaching. But to the older Indians, the men, the braves, as we would call them probably today, they didn't have much interest in anything else they had to memorize or to learn. But they were addicted to smoking, very heavy smokers they were. So Father DeSmeti hit upon a plan where he gave them an incentive and also a reward. He told them that if they learned a commandment, he would give them a pipe full of tobacco. And as they smoked, why, they learned a commandment. And the Indians soon called the 10 commandments the smoke of the 10 pipes. And it is still called that today amongst some of the Indians of the Portlands, and referring back to their ancient history, the smoke of the tin pipes. Oh, That's really interesting. Well, as Father DeSmit, when he returned back to the area in 1844, he was surprised at how well the people had learned his prayers when he had given them. The whole village could say the prayers that he taught the children. They could also say the Ten Commandments, which is indeed a very sacred thing and does cast a reflection upon the fact that the Jesuit priests, when they came to an area, they did not bring capital, Nor did they bring money, nor did they build roads, but they brought civilization. And if we then would like to move on a little further, we have had now the precinct of the area. Down along the Clearwater River, we'll go into that area there, which was also part of Shoshone County at that time. Now, a person should probably stop and realize the size of Shoshone County that was so bestrewed by Governor Isaac Engel Stevenson in the year of 1858. It compromised an area, the all land north of the Snake River as a southern boundary to the Canadian border. It took all the land to the east, to the Rockies, or any eastern border, and all the land to the westward, as of the west of the west coast, say, to the Columbia River, was made into a very large area, which was comprised of the state of Idaho, and others are made of Shoshone County. As a matter of fact, Shoshone County, as you can see, then was comprised of parts of the state of Montana, state of Washington, the state of Idaho. These are probably the largest, larger than the state of Idaho is today, in Baltimore County. Now, these are boundary lines we're not here to do. Because We find out that after the gold strike in there, in the Pearside around the 1860s, this land was being shifted around very rapidly. The boundary lines through the state of Washington moved in, and also the boundary lines with the, instead of being the Rockies, were changed to the Bitterroots. And the first thing you know, they wound up with a little narrow panhandle north side of them. And Most of that was still all Shoshone County, though. Well, after the Gold Strike of 1860, it brought many a thousand people into the area there. It was a period of time of turbulence in the United States. There was a great deal of dissatisfaction with regards to national policies of slavery. Also, the gold mines in California were being worked out. And There was also a great deal of dissatisfaction with the way new states were being admitted into the Union or new territories being declared as whether they should be slaves or not be slaves. And the result of this was that the United States government was not really putting its fullest efforts into the western part of the United States. It was not a full federal group, so it was taken back to the areas of conflict. So the result of it was that there was not too much law and order in the territory of Idaho, or we should say in Shoshone County. The stripe of gold was made at an Orofino brick. And the reason it had that name was oro meaning gold, and Spanish, fino meaning fine gold. This is the area of fine gold, the word Orofino. And it produced a great deal of wealth during this period. And also, at the same time, There was another gold discovery a little bit to the south of there, over in the Florence and Dixie area. That area there was called the Oro Grande. Of the Oro over there, it was gold coarse. That's why it was called Grande. Well, these mines, during the period of 1860 to approximately 1867, produced over $149 million in gold and had a very significant bearing on the outcome of the Civil War. And the gold supplies in California were gone by about that time. So you can see Shoshone County is a very active part of the early history of the United States. This area soon was depleted, worked out, and there wasn't much left in the area. The white people were leaving it. The Chinese took over. They were working the planes down there on the Clearwater. And they weren't doing too well either. But then we had another gold strike in North Idaho. Now it's the year of 1883. It was made by a man who was given credit for it. We'll say his name was Mr. Pritchard, who came into this area here in 1879. And he was rather an odd sort of a person. He belonged to an organization which called themselves the Free thinkers. And he had an idea that at some time he would establish a colony in this area if he could just find enough gold so it could be profitable. And he was made the first local location in Shoshone County, which is up to approximately down to Walgren, Idaho, where it is today. It's called Evolution. He'd also built a cattle there and a prospect, what was called a prospecting where the state barns are up there, their highway equipment. But it was in 1883 that he made this discovery. He was credited with it anyway. Although a lot of people were challenged that he was not the first old Peter Grubb, the first discovered. Also, the evolution claim is contested too, topping the first discovery was in the area. There was a party by the name of, I can't recall his name right now, the Schmelle Expedition, I think it was called. It came in here in 1865. And they located mining claims adjacent to, not too far, the Sacred Heart Mission. And there were 11 mining claims there, and they reported them to Kingston, Idaho. So we have a record of that. Also, there was a type of decline in Kingston, Idaho. We know that it was located in Bingham, Oregon at the time that Richard did his discoveries in Murray, Idaho. Because Adam Allback in the Morning Sun newspaper, which you can read in Murray, told us in 1883 how there was a road being built south of Kingston to build mining property in that vicinity. So now there would have to be a location right there, or have a road built there. And we also know that this mining property was purchased from the Northern Pacific Railroad and incorporated in the year 1887. So they were given challenges to some of the state of the state in regards to the pressure. The Indians that go back to the building of the Sacred Heart Mission, the work was started on that by the Jesuits there in the year of 1846 is completed about approximately, oh, some people say 1850 and some 1852. I don't know exactly for sure. But I think the paramount structure was completed though approximately about the year of 1850. In the meantime, the United States government had the controversy, as you might recall, of the settlement of the boundary lines between Canada and the United States. Also, there was the old hangover from the War of 1812, which had never been settled, the French democracy from behind the scenes. This resulted in the Treaty of Ghent. And this Treaty of Ghent, why, this Oregon Territory also, which was in question, at the same time, the boundary lines that had been established there. And the Indians were more or less, they went through a series of wars. They had what was called the War of 1858, Colonel Wright. We were four-lane Indians in Spokane and in the Yak Maws, various Indians in the northwest of Mark. They were sadly defeated. They were a part of white. And he was safely retreated. And the result of it was that in the western area, there was a need for transportation. It was one thing that was thought to be more essential. And transportation should be established. And a railroad should be built to the west coast. So Governor Isaac Angel Stevenson, who is later to come up, the character of Governor, in Washington, He was one of the party that went out to take an argument and explore an area 500 miles wide, he was, and try to find a route to the Northwest. Well, he wasted a great deal of his time in West Bank, the Indian treaty, which he had no authority to do. He was a little lax in his discipline. He allowed his subveyors and prospectors, subveyors, economists, parties, and prospectoria also to get through. He overspent the funds which he was allotted. And when he came over by the fire to Vitteru, so here is the location of the hilltop town, he found out that he had to get out of there. Winter was coming. And he better speed it up a little bit, hurry over here to his destination in the state of Washington, where the Tumwater is, which is the territory of the Capitol. Well, as Colonel Wright, I mean as Isaac Daniels Davis was coming out, well he left Lieutenant John Mullen on the Bitterroot River to study the winter. River to study the winter left there. And that was in the year of 1853. And then he followed up the Missoula River that was called the Clarkshore River down the Clarkshore or the Missoula River until he came to the St. Regis River. And as he followed the St. Regis River to its headwaters, he came up to the vicinity which is between the boundary line now of Idaho and Montana is called Lookout Pass. But he was not really in the present location of Lookout Pass. He did not cross Lookout Pass, he crossed the Soho Pass, which is further up the tributary of the St. Regis. There he climbed the high mountain peak which is named after him today, Stevens Peak, which is located down in Baltimore. And he remarked of what a wonderful view he had of the delightful lookout. And that is where we got the main lookout pass. And he noticed how on the Montana side, the road gradually, I mean the trails just gradually, went up on a gentle slope, but on the Idaho side they dropped right off of it. He made a very good location of that. He also told us, looking out into the Flackers' country, and seeing the things in that area. Undoubtedly, there were good Indian trails at that time, very good Indian trails. These Indians had been crossing this area for centuries, longer than anybody knows. Indians have no record of the time of how they went over to Montana to hunt buffalo or how they forced through this area. And up here at the headwaters of the Fondre Lake at Steamboat Landing there are some hieroglyphics there that were discovered by a government surveyor by the name of Leeburg in the year of 1890, that no Indian tribe can interpret, or no person can tell what they mean. And they're still there. Also, we know that following the mission point and taking to the divide between the Port Lane and the St. Joe River, there's a trail, Indian Trail, there, which is called the Monumental Trail. And Dr. Scott, the guide of his father, wrote the book, Shadowy St. Joe, said that when the government of Severus went through that, following that Indian trail, they found 15 monuments there, marking the trail, and that there was rut stuck there over two foot feet from the Indian foliage. And that there were a lot of wild horses in the area of Harrison Flats and things of that nature. And that Indians employed them to feed horses, undoubtedly. So we can see that there was well-established trails. So Isaac Engel Stevenson, he made the march from Lookout Pass to the Sacred Heart Mission in one day. Forty miles. You can see there was well-established Indian trails through this area. And when he neared the mission, he was about four miles from the mission, which is probably about the vicinity of Kingston. He said in his journals that he met some Indian boys herding some pigs back towards the mission. And that they were also, in the evening, the Indians' time to bury their dead, that they were having a mass for an Indian there which died and he was surprised at how well they could say the mass. And he also saw an Indian woman milking a cow. She had 28 milk cows. And he was surprised at the dexterity she had in milking because she used both hands. Now this same mission, there were Many, many famous people stopped there for this mission. They had to sign the card. There was one of them was Mr. General Sherman. He stopped there. I don't know why he's getting those people to stop there. He doesn't stop there. So this mission, though, was abandoned in 1877 by the Indians. It was taken off the reservation. It was not put into reservation in 1873. It was four years they left the nation. It was unable, it was a mission, really not in a suitable location to support agriculture or a very many people. There were a lot of people. There was a broad, fertile valley here, but it was subject to overflows and things of that nature also. So the result was then that we had the Indian Wars right after this, after Isaac Engel Stevenson. And Stevenson went back to Washington, D.C. And there he gained an appropriation for building this road of $45, 000 and Lieutenant John Bone was assigned to building this road. He was an officer in Wright's Army in this area. He was in command of about 30 Nez Perce scouts who were favorable to the American forces. And some of these Indians who were here at the Sacred Heart Mission, they'd been rather frightful, they were, Colonel Wright believed they'd hung some of the Indians down in Spokane, Indiana. He'd also killed all the Spokane Indians' horses. In fact, he slaughtered over 900 of them at approximately the location where the Washington State Highway Patrol building is situated now. This is where we crossed from Idaho into Washington. A few years ago you could see the bones there of the horses. They'd all gathered up the old soul. I remember seeing them, a lot of other people were carrying out secret boats there. And that was also the site of the Platts Ferry across the Spokane River. Well, after the Indian War settled in about 1859, right then is when John Longstock built his road to this area. And he built a mighty, mighty four-row tube. It's a good thing he didn't have to come back over again, because he couldn't have done it, I reckon. And the good thing he built it when he did because he couldn't have built it today. We know that he got run over with an automobile. But he marked his mileage, he did, after he came up from the south, he did Walla Walla, and he came up the Hangman Trail, the plate off right the other side of the Volcan. He built a bridge across the St. Joseph River, and it built a barge to come on up to the mission, the Sacred Heart. Then he used it as a depot practically. And then he marked his mileage as coming eastward, he did this from the miles from the Sacred Heart Mission. His first day he stopped right here in Smellerville. That was his first night. Nine miles he was from the mission. And this was known after that as Nine Mile Prairie here in Martinville, Alabama. Before that, it had been marked by a, the students in his mother's prairie. And, then he proceeded to go westward. Now, there's a lot of conjecture about people saying, well, about gold discoveries and things of that nature. They call it new and improved in this area and all that. But there was most of the people who make these references to that have their location and their dates very well mixed up. Because in a letter to the Western Publishing Company, which Paul wrote, which is in the copy of the History of North Idaho, he says that he was in the vicinity of Frenchtown, Montana when this man came in with the gold. He was not in the good lands. So the stories here get oftentimes embellished or stretched out a little bit. I've read many times where he was at this point. He was at that point. Also had an attorney in Calgary, a book would go ahead of him over here, and Cavett City, Montana, there, Captain Cone, one of these captains, at the moment he'd letter the U.S. Reconstruction Company in 1903, the history of North Idaho. He answers this very thoroughly. He tells the name of the man who brought the gold in. So that is on the means of transportation and was in this area at the gold strike of 1883 in Murray, Idaho. Also, the Northern Pacific Railway had built a railroad up the Clarks Fork River. And when they heard of the gold discovery in North Idaho, they put out a circular trying to bring people into their railroad area where they had built. And it was a fabulous lie. They told of wealth that nobody ever knew of, that the mines were richer than those of Proceus, and that there were veins of silver ore that led to streaking through the hills that we followed for miles. Told of all the great agricultural lands and the timber. How anyone of just the least amount of ambition if they went into the Portland and Mining Region would come out of there with a fortune. Well, they started the stampede all right. And the people came in here from various points. One of them, rather, coming in was leaving from Belmont, Montana, which was off the Northern Pacific Railway and coming over the mountains, through over by the Jack White area, Jack White Pine, down Eagle Creek. Another way was over from Townsend Falls, the prospect of moving the hill down into Murray. The other way was to follow the Mullen Road up through the area, or to come to Killam, which is called Ten Mile Prairie, up there, go by Jack Cass Prairie over the hills. There was also the high water trails up the Northport River, and the harbor. So, there was many ways to approach this area. And when these people came into this area, they came from all walks, all trades of life. Some of them were miners, A lot of them were just hanger-ons, secret reports, gamblers, prostitutes, any type of person you could think of, merchants, lawyers.

lines.line: > The speaker was born in Bergen, Norway, and discusses his early life and his father's migration to the United States. His father, a cook by profession, moved to the U.S. as a young man and was involved in the rush to Alaska and the mining district of Fort Benton around the turn of the century.

metadata.title: Introduction and Background
metadata.synopsis: The speaker was born in Bergen, Norway, and discusses his early life and his father's migration to the United States. His father, a cook by profession, moved to the U.S. as a young man and was involved in the rush to Alaska and the mining district of Fort Benton around the turn of the century.

lines.line: # Early Jesuit Missions and Interactions with Native Tribes

> The speaker discusses the early Jesuit missions in Northern Idaho, focusing on their interactions with the Coeur d'Alene Indians, who were initially hostile. The Jesuits, led by Father De Smet, established the first mission house and introduced the natives to Christianity, adapting their teaching methods to the local culture.

metadata.title: Early Jesuit Missions and Interactions with Native Tribes
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the early Jesuit missions in Northern Idaho, focusing on their interactions with the Coeur d'Alene Indians, who were initially hostile. The Jesuits, led by Father De Smet, established the first mission house and introduced the natives to Christianity, adapting their teaching methods to the local culture.

lines.line: # Development and Changes in Shoshone County

> The speaker describes the historical development of Shoshone County, emphasizing its large size and the shifting of boundary lines following gold discoveries. The county's boundaries encompassed parts of what are now Montana and Washington, reflecting the fluid nature of territorial claims during that period.

metadata.title: Development and Changes in Shoshone County
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the historical development of Shoshone County, emphasizing its large size and the shifting of boundary lines following gold discoveries. The county's boundaries encompassed parts of what are now Montana and Washington, reflecting the fluid nature of territorial claims during that period.

lines.line: > The speaker details the significant gold discoveries in the region, particularly at Orofino Creek and the subsequent gold rush that brought thousands of people, including miners and other fortune seekers. This influx had a profound impact on the local economy and played a role in the broader historical events, such as the Civil War.

metadata.title: Gold Discoveries and Economic Impact
metadata.synopsis: The speaker details the significant gold discoveries in the region, particularly at Orofino Creek and the subsequent gold rush that brought thousands of people, including miners and other fortune seekers. This influx had a profound impact on the local economy and played a role in the broader historical events, such as the Civil War.

lines.line: > The speaker concludes with further insights into the historical significance of the region, discussing the aftermath of the Indian Wars and the construction of the Mullan Road. He reflects on the enduring impact of these events on the development of Northern Idaho and the broader Northwestern United States.

metadata.title: Further Historical Insights and Conclusion
metadata.synopsis: The speaker concludes with further insights into the historical significance of the region, discussing the aftermath of the Indian Wars and the construction of the Mullan Road. He reflects on the enduring impact of these events on the development of Northern Idaho and the broader Northwestern United States.

His father, a cook by profession, moved to the U.S. as a young man and was involved in the rush to Alaska and the mining district of Fort Benton around the turn of the century.
# Early Jesuit Missions and Interactions with Native Tribes

> The speaker discusses the early Jesuit missions in Northern Idaho, focusing on their interactions with the Coeur d'Alene Indians, who were initially hostile. The Jesuits, led by Father De Smet, established the first mission house and introduced the natives to Christianity, adapting their teaching methods to the local culture.
# Development and Changes in Shoshone County

> The speaker describes the historical development of Shoshone County, emphasizing its large size and the shifting of boundary lines following gold discoveries. The county's boundaries encompassed parts of what are now Montana and Washington, reflecting the fluid nature of territorial claims during that period.

> The speaker details the significant gold discoveries in the region, particularly at Orofino Creek and the subsequent gold rush that brought thousands of people, including miners and other fortune seekers. This influx had a profound impact on the local economy and played a role in the broader historical events, such as the Civil War.

> The speaker concludes with further insights into the historical significance of the region, discussing the aftermath of the Indian Wars and the construction of the Mullan Road. He reflects on the enduring impact of these events on the development of Northern Idaho and the broader Northwestern United States.
